explanation: The property states that globally (G), if the power button is pressed (bool_exp1), then eventually (F) the final state will be reached (bool_exp3). This captures the requirement that pushing the power button is the means by which the system reaches its final state.

The property states that globally, if the power button is pressed, then eventually the system will reach the final state and be off. This directly translates the requirement that the final state is reached by pushing the power button to turn it off.

The property states that globally, whenever the power button is pressed, it eventually leads to the system reaching the final state. The phrase "to turn it off" describes the intended immediate consequence of pressing the power button, which ultimately results in the system entering the final state.

The property states that globally (G), if the power button (bool_exp1) is pressed, then eventually (F) the system will be in the off state (bool_exp3). This directly captures the requirement that pushing the power button leads to the system being turned off, which is the 'Final State' in this context.

The property states that globally, if the power button is pressed (bool_exp1), then eventually (F) the system will reach the final state (bool_exp3). This captures the requirement that pushing the power button is the means to achieve the final state, which in this context means turning the unit off.

This LTL property ensures that the system reaches the 'final state' and turns 'off' as a result of pressing the power button. The first part, 'G (((! powerButton) & (X powerButton)) -> (X (F (finalState & off))))', means that whenever the power button is pressed (i.e., transitions from not pressed to pressed), then in the very next moment, the system must eventually reach the final state and be off. The second part, '(powerButton -> (F (finalState & off)))', ensures that if the power button is already pressed at any given time, the system will eventually transition to the final state and be off. This captures the intended behavior of reaching a final, off state upon pushing the power button.

The property states that the final state should be reached by the action of pushing the power button. This is captured by ensuring that, globally, if the power button is pressed (either it just became pressed or it is already pressed), then the final state will eventually be reached. The first part of the LTL formula handles the event of the button being pushed (transition from not pressed to pressed), while the second part ensures that if the button is currently pressed, the final state will still eventually be reached. 'To turn it off' specifies that the final state is effectively the 'off' state, which is directly represented by the 'finalState' atomic proposition.

The property states that globally (G), if the power button is pressed (bool_exp2), then eventually (F) the final state will be reached (bool_exp3). This captures the requirement that pushing the power button leads to the system entering the final state.

The property states that globally, if the power button is pushed (bool_exp2), then eventually the final state will be reached (bool_exp3). This captures that the final state is to be reached by the action of pushing the power button.

The natural language describes a response property: if a specific action (pushing the power button) occurs, then a certain state (final state) must eventually be reached. Template 19, 'G (bool_exp2 -> (F bool_exp3))', perfectly captures this 'always if trigger then eventually consequence' pattern. 'bool_exp2' is set to 'powerButton' as it is the action that initiates the state change, and 'bool_exp3' is set to 'finalState' as it is the desired state to be reached. The N_DURATION is null because no specific time bound is mentioned for reaching the final state.
